title: Convolution vs. Fully-Connected Layers
concept: Convolutions are far more efficient than fully-connected layers for images because of parameter sharing — the same small filter is reused across the entire image — and local connectivity — each output only looks at a small neighborhood of pixels, instead of every pixel connecting to every neuron.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If a convolution layer has 16 filters, what does its output look like?

options.text: A stack of 16 separate feature maps, one per filter
16 unrelated copies of the raw input image

exercises.explain: Right. Now let's see why this setup is so much cheaper than the alternative.

cards.title: Why not just connect every pixel to every neuron?
cards.body: You've learned about neurons that connect to every input — that's a fully-connected layer. So why do CNNs bother with sliding filters instead of just connecting every pixel straight to a neuron? The answer is about to save millions of numbers.

cards.title: Two very different ways to connect
cards.body: A fully-connected layer gives every single pixel its own connection to every neuron, each with its own learned weight. A convolution layer instead reuses one small filter, sliding it across the whole image — this is called parameter sharing, an idea you first met back in Unit 87.

cards.title: One stencil vs. a custom brush for every inch
cards.body: A fully-connected layer is like hand-painting a giant mural with a totally custom brushstroke for every single square inch — exhausting, and each inch never benefits from what you learned painting the last one. A convolution is like using one stencil and stamping it across the entire mural — learn the stencil once, reuse it everywhere.

cards.title: The numbers get big, fast
cards.body: A small 100x100 pixel image fully connected to just 100 neurons needs about a million separate weights, one for every pixel-to-neuron pair. A convolution layer solving a similar problem might use a handful of tiny filters, as few as 9 numbers each, reused at every single position in the image.

cards.title: Fewer parameters doesn't mean weaker
cards.body: It's tempting to think fewer numbers means a worse model. It's the opposite for images — local connectivity, where each output only looks at a small nearby patch instead of the whole image, actually matches how real-world patterns work: an edge or a corner is a local feature with no need to connect to distant pixels.

cards.title: Fully-connected vs. convolution
cards.body: Two very different tradeoffs for the same image:

left.text:
Every pixel wired to
every neuron, huge parameter count

right.text:
One small filter reused
everywhere, few parameters, local view

exercises.prompt: What is 'parameter sharing' in a convolution layer?

options.text: The same small filter's numbers are reused at every position as it slides across the image
Every neuron gets a completely unique, independent set of weights
Parameters are copied between unrelated neural networks
The filter's numbers change every time it slides to a new position

exercises.explain: Right — one learned filter, reused everywhere it slides.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a fully-connected layer typically needs far more weights to process an image than a convolution layer does.
exercises.explain: True. Wiring every pixel to every neuron adds up fast, while a shared filter stays small no matter how big the image is.

exercises.sentence: Because a convolution filter only looks at a small nearby patch of pixels instead of the entire image at once, this property is called ___ connectivity.
exercises.explain: Local is right — each output only depends on a small neighborhood of pixels.

exercises.prompt: Which setup is more parameter-efficient for finding edges across a large photo?

options.text: A small filter reused at every position across the image
A fully-connected layer with a unique weight for every pixel-to-neuron pair
Storing a separate copy of the entire image for every neuron

exercises.explain: Right — reusing one small filter beats wiring up a brand-new weight for every pixel.

exercises.prompt: A team is choosing between a fully-connected layer and a convolution layer to process 1-megapixel photos. Which choice makes more sense, and why?

options.text: Convolution — parameter sharing and local connectivity mean far fewer weights are needed, and the same learned filter can still detect a pattern no matter where it appears in the image
Fully-connected — more individual weights always means a smarter model
Convolution — because it deletes most of the pixels before processing them
Fully-connected — because convolutions can only work on grayscale images

exercises.explain: Exactly right — that efficiency is why every image-processing CNN is built from convolutions, not fully-connected layers. Unit complete! Next up: stride and padding — how far the filter jumps each step, and what happens at the edges of the image.
